1:
"And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan." The witnesses note Isaac blessing Jacob and forbidding a Canaanite wife. On "Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him," the grammatical voices note the deliberate blessing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him" — Isaac now, deliberately and knowingly (no longer deceived, ch. 27), blessed Jacob and charged him; the patriarch confirming the blessing to Jacob with open eyes; the contrast with the stolen blessing (ch. 27) — here Isaac freely and consciously blesses the heir; the deliberate conveying of the covenant.

On "blessed him, and charged him," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the confirmation. Henry and Calvin note that Isaac, having acquiesced in God's purpose (27:33), now intentionally blessed Jacob (vv.3-4) and charged him; the patriarch ratifying the blessing; the acceptance of God's choice of Jacob.

On "Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the charge: "Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan" — Isaac charged Jacob not to marry a Canaanite (as Esau had, 26:34-35, to his parents' grief, v.46); the care to keep the covenant-line from the accursed, idolatrous Canaanites (24:3); the marriage in the faith; the charge that sent Jacob to the kindred (v.2). The verse displays Isaac blessing Jacob and forbidding a Canaanite wife.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him," the deliberate blessing (ch. 27, Keil, Gill); "blessed him, and charged him," the conscious confirming of the blessing (27:33, Henry, Calvin); "Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan," the charge against a Canaanite wife (24:3; 26:34-35, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Isaac. The verse marks the blessing: deliberate. The verse displays the charge: no Canaanite wife. The verse displays the covenant: confirmed. The verse displays the marriage: in the faith. The blessing displays Isaac freely confirming the heir. The verse displays the Isaac: blessing. The verse displays the Jacob: charged. The verse displays the Canaanites: forbidden. Isaac blessing Jacob and forbidding a Canaanite wife displays the deliberate conveying of the covenant: Isaac blessed Jacob and charged him not to take a wife of Canaan — the patriarch now consciously (ch. 27) confirming the blessing to the heir, and keeping the covenant-line from the Canaanites (24:3). The verse displays the blessing Isaac, who sends Jacob to the kindred (v.2).
2:
"Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother's father; and take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban thy mother's brother." The witnesses note Isaac sending Jacob to the kindred for a wife. On "Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother's father," the grammatical voices note the journey. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother's father" — Isaac sent Jacob to Padan-aram (Mesopotamia, the kindred), to the house of Bethuel (Rebekah's father, 24:24); the journey to the covenant family; the seeking of a wife among the kindred (as Abraham for Isaac, ch. 24).

On "take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the wife. Calvin and Gill note that Jacob was to take a wife "of the daughters of Laban thy mother's brother" — from the family of Laban (Rebekah's brother, 24:29); the wife of the kindred and faith; the covenant-marriage within the family (as Isaac's, ch. 24); the providence directing Jacob to Rachel (29:10-11).

On the sending, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the providence: Isaac's sending Jacob to Padan-aram served both for a wife and (by Rebekah's design, 27:43-45) for safety from Esau; the providence ordering the exile that would build the house of Israel (chs. 29-30); the journey to the kindred. The verse displays Isaac sending Jacob to the kindred.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel," Jacob sent to the kindred (24:24, Keil, Gill); "take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban," the wife of the family (24:29, Calvin, Gill); the providence ordering the journey (27:43-45, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sending Isaac. The verse marks the journey: to Padan-aram. The verse displays the house: of Bethuel. The verse displays the wife: of Laban's daughters. The verse displays the kindred: sought. The sending displays Isaac directing Jacob to the covenant family. The verse displays the Isaac: sending. The verse displays the Jacob: to the kindred. The verse displays the wife: of the faith. Isaac sending Jacob to the kindred for a wife displays the covenant-marriage within the family: go to Padan-aram, to Bethuel's house, and take a wife of Laban's daughters (24:24, 29) — the heir sent to the kindred for a wife of the faith (as Isaac, ch. 24), the journey providence would overrule (chs. 29-30). The verse displays the sent Jacob, blessed with the blessing of Abraham (vv.3-4).
3:
"And God Almighty bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, that thou mayest be a multitude of people;" The witnesses note Isaac's blessing of fruitfulness in the name of God Almighty. On "God Almighty bless thee," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "God Almighty (El Shaddai) bless thee" — Isaac blessed Jacob in the name of God Almighty (El Shaddai, the name by which God had revealed himself to Abraham in the covenant, 17:1); the God of the covenant invoked; the blessing grounded on the Almighty's power to fulfill it; the covenant-name marking the covenant-blessing.

On "make thee fruitful, and multiply thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the fruitfulness. Calvin and Gill note "make thee fruitful, and multiply thee" — the blessing of a numerous posterity (as promised to Abraham, 17:6); the multiplying of Jacob's seed; the covenant-promise of fruitfulness conveyed to the heir.

On "that thou mayest be a multitude of people," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the nation: "that thou mayest be a multitude of people" — Jacob to become a multitude (a company of peoples, the nation of Israel, 35:11, a nation and a company of nations); the covenant-promise of a great people; the blessing of Abraham (17:4-6) conveyed to Jacob. The verse displays Isaac's blessing of fruitfulness in the name of God Almighty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "God Almighty (El Shaddai) bless thee," the covenant-name invoked (17:1, Ellicott, Gill); "make thee fruitful, and multiply thee," the blessing of posterity (17:6, Calvin, Gill); "that thou mayest be a multitude of people," the nation of Israel (35:11, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Isaac. The verse marks the name: God Almighty. The verse displays the fruitfulness: blessed. The verse displays the multitude: a people. The verse displays the covenant: invoked. The blessing displays Isaac conveying the promise of fruitfulness. The verse displays the Isaac: blessing. The verse displays the Jacob: made fruitful. The verse displays the nation: promised. Isaac's blessing of fruitfulness in the name of God Almighty displays the covenant-promise conveyed: God Almighty (El Shaddai, 17:1) make thee fruitful and a multitude of people — the blessing of a numerous posterity (17:6; 35:11), the promise of Abraham conveyed to Jacob in the covenant-name. The verse displays the blessing Isaac, who gives the blessing of Abraham (v.4).
4:
"And give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee; that thou mayest inherit the land wherein thou art a stranger, which God gave unto Abraham." The witnesses note the blessing of Abraham conveyed to Jacob — the crux of the covenant-blessing. On "give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee," interpreters expound the blessing. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading note that Isaac prayed God to "give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed" — the full covenant-blessing made to Abraham (12:2-3; 17:4-8; 22:17-18) now consciously and deliberately conveyed to Jacob (and his seed); Clarke notes that "the blessing of Abraham, which is to come upon" the heir, is confirmed; the covenant — the land, the seed, and the blessing of all nations (and supremely the Messianic line, the seed in whom all families are blessed, v.14) — bestowed on Jacob as the chosen heir; Isaac, now with open eyes (contrast ch. 27), freely ratifying God's choice (Hebrews 11:21, by faith Isaac blessed Jacob).

On "that thou mayest inherit the land wherein thou art a stranger," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the land. Gill notes that the blessing included the inheritance of "the land wherein thou art a stranger" (Canaan, the land of promise, where Jacob, like Abraham, sojourned as a stranger, Hebrews 11:9, 13); the covenant-inheritance of Canaan conveyed to Jacob and his seed; the land promised to Abraham (13:15; 17:8) now to Jacob.

On "which God gave unto Abraham," the grammatical-historical note the continuity: "which God gave unto Abraham" — the land and blessing given to Abraham (17:8) now confirmed to Jacob; the covenant continued in the chosen line (narrowed from Abraham through Isaac to Jacob); the blessing of Abraham the heritage of the heir. The verse displays the blessing of Abraham conveyed to Jacob.

The witnesses agree the substance: Isaac conveyed to Jacob the blessing of Abraham — the land, the seed, and the covenant (Clarke, Gill). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of the covenant-blessing: "the blessing of Abraham" (12:2-3; 17:4-8; 22:17-18) — the full covenant (the land of Canaan, the numberless seed, and the blessing of all nations in the seed, supremely Christ) consciously and deliberately conveyed to Jacob as the chosen heir; Isaac, with open eyes, ratifying God's choice by faith (Hebrews 11:21). The verse displays the covenant-blessing crux. The verse marks the blessing: of Abraham. The verse displays the seed: with thee. The verse displays the land: inherited. The verse displays the covenant: conveyed. The blessing displays the Abrahamic covenant given to Jacob. The verse displays the Jacob: heir. The verse displays the land: of promise. The verse displays the covenant: continued. The blessing of Abraham conveyed to Jacob displays the crux: give thee the blessing of Abraham, that thou mayest inherit the land which God gave unto Abraham (17:8) — the full covenant (the land, the seed, the blessing of all nations) consciously conveyed to Jacob the chosen heir, Isaac ratifying God's choice by faith (Hebrews 11:21). The verse displays the blessed Jacob, sent on his way (v.5).
5:
"And Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian, the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother." The witnesses note Jacob's departure to Padan-aram. The grammatical voices note the sending. Ellicott and Gill note that "Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian" — Isaac sent Jacob away (with the blessing, vv.3-4, and the charge, vv.1-2), and Jacob set out for Padan-aram, to Laban (Rebekah's brother, 24:29; the Syrian/Aramean); the departure of the heir; the journey to the kindred (and into exile).

On "unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian," the witnesses note the kindred. Gill notes that Jacob went to Laban (the brother of Rebekah, the family of Nahor, 22:20-23) — the covenant kindred; the place of the wife and the long sojourn (chs. 29-31).

On "the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the connection: "the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother" — the careful noting of the family relation (Laban the uncle); the providence sending Jacob to the kindred; the journey that fulfilled both the charge (a wife of the kindred) and the flight (from Esau, 27:43); the exile of the heir. The verse displays Jacob's departure to Padan-aram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sending — "Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban," Jacob's departure (vv.3-4, Ellicott, Gill); "unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian," the kindred (24:29, Gill); "the brother of Rebekah," the family connection (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departing Jacob. The verse marks the sending: away. The verse displays the Padan-aram: his journey. The verse displays the Laban: the kindred. The verse displays the exile: begun. The departure displays Jacob sent to the kindred. The verse displays the Isaac: sending. The verse displays the Jacob: departing. The verse displays the Laban: his uncle. Jacob's departure to Padan-aram displays the journey to the kindred and into exile: Isaac sent Jacob to Padan-aram, to Laban, Rebekah's brother (24:29) — the heir sent with the blessing (vv.3-4) to the covenant kindred, the exile that fulfilled both the charge and the flight (27:43). The verse displays the journeying Jacob, before Esau's marriage (vv.6-9).
6:
"When Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram, to take him a wife from thence; and that as he blessed him he gave him a charge, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan;" The witnesses note Esau observing the blessing and charge given to Jacob. On "When Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram," the grammatical voices note the observation. Ellicott and Gill note that "Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram, to take him a wife" — Esau observed that Jacob was blessed and sent to the kindred for a wife; the elder noting the favor and the charge; the occasion of his own (misjudged) attempt to please his parents (v.9).

On "as he blessed him he gave him a charge... Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the charge. Henry and Calvin note that Esau saw the charge — that Jacob was not to marry a Canaanite (v.1); the elder perceiving that the Canaanite marriages were displeasing; the observation that prompted his attempt to mend matters (v.9).

On the observation, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note Esau's reasoning: Esau, seeing that the Canaanite wives (his own, 26:34) grieved his parents, sought to please them by another marriage (v.9); the carnal man's misjudged remedy (marrying an Ishmaelite, still outside the covenant-line); the profane Esau trying to win favor by an outward act. The verse displays Esau observing the blessing and charge given to Jacob.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the observation — "Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away... to take him a wife," Esau noting the favor and charge (Ellicott, Gill); "Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan," the charge observed (v.1, Henry, Calvin); the occasion of Esau's attempt to please his parents (v.9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the observing Esau. The verse marks the blessing: of Jacob. The verse displays the charge: no Canaanite wife. The verse displays the Esau: observing. The verse displays the remedy: misjudged. The observation displays Esau perceiving the displeasure at Canaanite wives. The verse displays the Esau: seeing. The verse displays the charge: noted. The verse displays the remedy: sought. Esau observing the blessing and charge given to Jacob displays his perception and his misjudged remedy: Esau saw Jacob blessed and sent to the kindred, charged not to marry a Canaanite (v.1) — the elder, perceiving his Canaanite wives grieved his parents (26:34-35), seeking to please them (v.9). The verse displays the observing Esau, who marries an Ishmaelite (vv.8-9).
7:
"And that Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram;" The very brief verse notes Jacob's obedience to his parents. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram" — Esau also observed that Jacob had obeyed his parents (Isaac's charge, v.1, and Rebekah's plan, 27:43) and gone to Padan-aram; the obedience of Jacob (to both father and mother); the contrast with Esau's self-willed marriages (26:34); the heir's compliance.

On "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother," the reformed and witnesses note the compliance. Gill notes Jacob's obedience to both parents — the dutiful son going as charged; the contrast with Esau (who pleased himself, 26:34); the obedience that Esau noted (and tried to imitate, v.9).

On "was gone to Padanaram," the grammatical-historical note the departure: "was gone to Padanaram" — Jacob already gone to the kindred (v.5); the obedience accomplished; the observation that moved Esau to his own attempt (v.9); the heir on his way. The verse displays Jacob's obedience to his parents.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram," Jacob's obedience and departure (v.1; 27:43, Keil, Gill); "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother," the dutiful compliance (Gill); "was gone to Padanaram," the heir on his way (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient Jacob. The verse marks the obedience: to both parents. The verse displays the departure: to Padan-aram. The verse displays the contrast: with Esau. The verse displays the heir: compliant. The obedience displays Jacob the dutiful son. The verse displays the Jacob: obeying. The verse displays the parents: heeded. The verse displays the journey: accomplished. Jacob's obedience to his parents displays the dutiful heir: Jacob obeyed his father and mother and was gone to Padan-aram (v.1; 27:43) — the contrast with Esau's self-willed marriages (26:34), the obedience Esau noted and tried to imitate (v.9). The verse displays the obedient Jacob, prompting Esau's marriage (vv.8-9).
8:
"And Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father;" The very brief verse notes Esau perceiving the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac. The grammatical voices note the perception. Gill notes that "Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father" — Esau now perceived (what he should have seen before) that the Canaanite women (his own wives, 26:34-35) displeased his father; the belated realization; the elder seeing the cause of the grief (26:35); the occasion of his attempt to mend matters (v.9).

On "the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father," the witnesses note the displeasure. Gill notes that the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac (and Rebekah, 26:35) — the grief of the godly parents; the cause Esau now perceived; the displeasure that prompted his new marriage.

On the perception, the grammatical-historical note the misjudgment: Esau's perceiving the displeasure led him to marry an Ishmaelite (v.9) — yet still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael, not the chosen seed); the carnal man's misjudged remedy; the attempt to please his parents by an outward act, without true understanding of the covenant; the profane Esau's mistaken correction. The verse displays Esau perceiving the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the perception — "Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father," Esau's belated realization (26:35, Gill); "the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac," the displeasure (Gill); the misjudged remedy (v.9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the perceiving Esau. The verse marks the perception: the displeasure. The verse displays the Canaanites: his wives. The verse displays the Isaac: displeased. The verse displays the remedy: misjudged. The perception displays Esau seeing the cause of the grief. The verse displays the Esau: perceiving. The verse displays the wives: displeasing. The verse displays the remedy: sought. Esau perceiving the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac displays his belated realization: Esau saw that the daughters of Canaan pleased not his father (26:35) — the elder perceiving the cause of his parents' grief, seeking (misjudgedly) to mend it (v.9). The verse displays the perceiving Esau, who takes an Ishmaelite wife (v.9).
9:
"Then went Esau unto Ishmael, and took unto the wives which he had Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael Abraham's son, the sister of Nebajoth, to be his wife." The witnesses note Esau marrying an Ishmaelite — the misjudged remedy. On "Then went Esau unto Ishmael, and took... Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael," the grammatical voices note the marriage. Ellicott and Gill note that "Esau went unto Ishmael, and took... Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael Abraham's son, the sister of Nebajoth" — Esau, to please his parents (vv.6-8), took a third wife from the family of Ishmael (Abraham's son, 25:12-16; Mahalath, sister of Nebajoth); the attempt to marry within the wider family of Abraham (not a Canaanite); the misjudged remedy.

On "unto the wives which he had," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the addition. Clarke and Gill note that Esau took Mahalath "unto the wives which he had" (26:34) — adding a third wife (not putting away the Canaanites); the polygamy; the half-measure that did not truly mend the fault (the Canaanite wives kept).

On the marriage, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the misjudgment: Esau's marrying an Ishmaelite — though within Abraham's wider family — was still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael, the son of the bondwoman, cast out, 21:10-12, not the chosen seed); the carnal man's misjudged attempt to please his parents by an outward, half-right act, without understanding the covenant (the chosen line in Isaac and Jacob, not Ishmael); the profane Esau's mistaken correction; the marriage that did not mend matters. The verse displays Esau marrying an Ishmaelite.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the marriage — "Esau went unto Ishmael, and took... Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael," Esau marrying within Abraham's wider family (25:12-16, Ellicott, Gill); "unto the wives which he had," the third wife added (26:34, Clarke, Gill); the misjudged remedy (Ishmael outside the covenant-line, 21:10-12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marrying Esau. The verse marks the wife: Ishmael's daughter. The verse displays the addition: a third wife. The verse displays the remedy: misjudged. The verse displays the covenant: not understood. The marriage displays Esau's mistaken attempt to please his parents. The verse displays the Esau: marrying. The verse displays the Ishmaelite: his wife. The verse displays the remedy: half-right. Esau marrying an Ishmaelite displays the misjudged remedy: Esau took Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter (25:13), to his Canaanite wives (26:34) — the carnal man's mistaken attempt to please his parents by an outward act, still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael cast out, 21:10-12), the marriage that did not mend matters. The verse closes Esau's history here, before Jacob's vision (vv.10-22).
10:
"And Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran." The witnesses note Jacob setting out alone from Beersheba toward Haran. The grammatical voices note the departure. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran" — Jacob set out from Beersheba (his father's dwelling, 26:23) toward Haran (Padan-aram, the kindred, vv.2, 5); the lone traveler (without retinue, unlike Abraham's servant, ch. 24); the fugitive heir on his long journey; the setting for the vision (vv.11-15).

On "went out from Beersheba," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the leaving. Henry and Gill note that Jacob left Beersheba (the place of the covenant and worship, 26:23-25) — going out alone, a stranger and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13); the heir leaving home and the land of promise (for a time); the lonely departure.

On "went toward Haran," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the journey: "went toward Haran" — the long journey to the kindred (vv.2, 5); the fugitive (from Esau, 27:43) and pilgrim; the providence ordering the way (and the vision at Bethel, vv.11-15) to comfort and assure the lonely traveler. The verse displays Jacob setting out alone toward Haran.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the departure — "Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran," Jacob's lone journey (26:23; vv.2, 5, Ellicott, Gill); "went out from Beersheba," the leaving of home (Hebrews 11:13, Henry, Gill); "went toward Haran," the journey to the kindred (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Jacob. The verse marks the departure: from Beersheba. The verse displays the journey: toward Haran. The verse displays the traveler: alone. The verse displays the vision: to come. The departure displays Jacob the lone pilgrim on his way. The verse displays the Jacob: going out. The verse displays the Beersheba: left. The verse displays the Haran: his goal. Jacob setting out alone from Beersheba toward Haran displays the fugitive heir on his journey: Jacob went out from Beersheba toward Haran (26:23; vv.2, 5) — the lone traveler and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), the setting for the vision that would comfort him (vv.11-15). The verse displays the journeying Jacob, who lodges at Bethel (v.11).
11:
"And he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night, because the sun was set; and he took of the stones of that place, and put them for his pillows, and lay down in that place to sleep." The witnesses note Jacob lodging at the place with a stone for a pillow. On "he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night, because the sun was set," the grammatical voices note the lodging. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night, because the sun was set" — Jacob, overtaken by nightfall, lodged at a certain place (later Bethel, v.19); the lone traveler resting in the open; the providence (the "certain place" where God would meet him); the humble lodging of the heir.

On "he took of the stones of that place, and put them for his pillows," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the pillow. Clarke and Ellicott note that Jacob took stones for his pillow (his head-rest) — the hard lodging of the pilgrim; the heir of the covenant sleeping on the ground with a stone for a pillow; the humble circumstances; the contrast with the glory of the vision to come (vv.12-15).

On "lay down in that place to sleep," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the setting: "lay down in that place to sleep" — Jacob slept; the setting for the dream (vv.12-15); the lonely, weary traveler about to be visited by God; the providence preparing the great vision; the place that would become Bethel (v.19). The verse displays Jacob lodging with a stone for a pillow.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lodging — "he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night," Jacob lodging at nightfall (v.19, Keil, Gill); "he took of the stones... and put them for his pillows," the hard pillow of the pilgrim (Clarke, Ellicott); "lay down in that place to sleep," the setting for the dream (vv.12-15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lodging Jacob. The verse marks the place: certain. The verse displays the night: fallen. The verse displays the stone: a pillow. The verse displays the dream: to come. The lodging displays Jacob the humble pilgrim resting. The verse displays the Jacob: lodging. The verse displays the stone: his pillow. The verse displays the place: Bethel-to-be. Jacob lodging at the place with a stone for a pillow displays the humble setting of the vision: Jacob lodged at a certain place, a stone for his pillow, and slept (v.19) — the lone, weary pilgrim about to be visited by God, the place that would become Bethel. The verse displays the sleeping Jacob, who dreams of the ladder (v.12).
12:
"And he dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the angels of God ascending and descending on it." The witnesses note the dream of the ladder — the crux of its signification. On "behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven," interpreters expound the vision. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the grammatical-historical note that Jacob dreamed of a ladder (or stairway) set on the earth and reaching to heaven; among the many interpretations, the chief and consistent are: (a) the providence of God, by which he watches over and governs his people on earth from heaven (the connection between heaven and earth, God's care reaching down to the lonely fugitive); (b) the ministry of angels (the angels ascending and descending) — the communion of heaven and earth, the angels sent forth to minister to the heirs of salvation (Hebrews 1:14), watching over Jacob; and (c), supremely, the type of Christ — the ladder a figure of Christ, in whom heaven and earth, God and man, are joined; "Jesus Christ is the grand connecting medium between heaven and earth, between God and man."

On the ladder as the type of Christ, the dominant Christian reading appeals to the Lord's own word: Jesus said to Nathanael, "Hereafter ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of man" (John 1:51) — applying the ladder to himself; Christ the Mediator (1 Timothy 2:5), the way (John 14:6), the union of God and man, by whom alone heaven is opened and communion restored; the ladder thus a gospel-figure of the incarnate Christ, in whom both worlds meet.

On "the angels of God ascending and descending on it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ministry: the angels ascending and descending show the constant ministry of the holy angels (ascending with the prayers and needs of the saints, descending with God's help and protection); the communion of heaven and earth; the comfort to the lonely Jacob that he was not forsaken, but watched over by God and his angels. The verse displays the dream of the ladder.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob saw a ladder joining earth and heaven, with the angels ascending and descending (Clarke, Calvin). The ladder bears its weight as a crux of its signification: the providence of God watching over his people; the ministry of the angels (the communion of heaven and earth); and, supremely, the type of Christ, the Mediator, in whom heaven and earth, God and man, are joined (John 1:51) — the ladder a gospel-figure of the incarnate Christ. The verse displays the ladder crux. The verse marks the ladder: earth to heaven. The verse displays the debate: providence, the angels, and the type of Christ. The verse displays the angels: ascending. The verse displays the Christ: the Mediator. The vision displays heaven and earth joined. The verse displays the ladder: set up. The verse displays the heaven: reached. The verse displays the Christ: foreshadowed. The dream of the ladder displays a crux: a ladder set on earth reaching to heaven, the angels ascending and descending — read as the providence of God, the ministry of angels (the communion of heaven and earth), and, supremely, the type of Christ the Mediator, in whom God and man are joined (John 1:51). The verse displays the dreaming Jacob, with the LORD above (vv.13-15).
13:
"And, behold, the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac: the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed;" The witnesses note the LORD above the ladder renewing the covenant of the land. On "the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Gill and Calvin note that "the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac" — the LORD (Jehovah) stood above the ladder and revealed himself as the covenant-God of the fathers (the God of Abraham and Isaac, Exodus 3:6); the covenant-God appearing to Jacob (as to Abraham and Isaac); the assurance that the God of his fathers was his God; the renewal of the covenant to the third patriarch.

On "the God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the covenant-God. Calvin and Gill note the title — "the LORD God of Abraham... and the God of Isaac" (the God of the covenant, the fathers' God); the continuity of the covenant; the God who had blessed Abraham and Isaac now confirming the blessing to Jacob.

On "the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the land: "the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed" — the LORD renewed the promise of the land (Canaan, where Jacob lay) to him and his seed (13:15; 17:8; 26:3); the covenant-inheritance confirmed to Jacob; the land of promise given to the heir and his posterity. The verse displays the LORD renewing the covenant of the land.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the revelation — "the LORD stood above it... I am the LORD God of Abraham... and the God of Isaac," the covenant-God appearing (Exodus 3:6, Gill, Calvin); "the God of Abraham... and the God of Isaac," the fathers' God (Calvin, Gill); "the land... to thee will I give it, and to thy seed," the land renewed (13:15; 17:8, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewing LORD. The verse marks the LORD: above the ladder. The verse displays the God: of Abraham and Isaac. The verse displays the land: given. The verse displays the covenant: renewed. The revelation displays the covenant-God renewing the promise to Jacob. The verse displays the LORD: revealing. The verse displays the Jacob: assured. The verse displays the land: promised. The LORD above the ladder renewing the covenant of the land displays the covenant-God's appearing: I am the LORD God of Abraham and Isaac; the land whereon thou liest I will give to thee and thy seed (Exodus 3:6; 13:15) — the covenant-God of the fathers confirming the land-promise to Jacob the heir. The verse displays the revealing LORD, who promises the seed and the blessing (v.14).
14:
"And thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south: and in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed." The witnesses note the promise of the seed and the blessing of all families renewed to Jacob. On "thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad," the grammatical voices note the seed. Gill and Clarke note that "thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south" — the LORD renewed the promise of a numberless seed (as the dust, 13:16; the stars, 15:5) and the spreading abroad (in every direction) of Jacob's posterity; the covenant-promise of multiplication and expansion; Israel to fill the land and spread.

On "in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed," the reformed and witnesses note the gospel. Gill notes the great gospel promise — "in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed" (12:3; 22:18; 26:4); the blessing of all families/nations in Jacob's seed; Paul declares the seed to be Christ (Galatians 3:16), in whom all nations are blessed; the covenant of grace, the gospel, confirmed to Jacob.

On the promise, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the continuity: the seed, the spreading, and the blessing of all families — the full covenant-promise (made to Abraham and Isaac, 12:3; 26:4) renewed entire to Jacob; the line of the Messiah (the seed, Christ) confirmed in him; the gospel-promise the heritage of the heir. The verse displays the promise of the seed and the blessing renewed to Jacob.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the seed — "thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad," the numberless, spreading seed (13:16, Gill, Clarke); "in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed," the gospel promise (12:3; Galatians 3:16, Gill); the covenant renewed entire (26:4, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised seed. The verse marks the seed: as the dust. The verse displays the spreading: every way. The verse displays the families: all blessed. The verse displays the gospel: confirmed. The promise displays the covenant renewed entire to Jacob. The verse displays the seed: multiplied. The verse displays the nations: in the seed. The verse displays the Christ: foreshadowed. The promise of the seed and the blessing of all families renewed to Jacob displays the gospel confirmed: thy seed as the dust, spreading every way, and in thy seed all families blessed (13:16; 12:3; Galatians 3:16) — the full covenant-promise renewed to Jacob, the line of the Messiah (the seed, Christ) confirmed in him. The verse displays the blessed Jacob, assured of God's keeping (v.15).
15:
"And, behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land; for I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of." The witnesses note the LORD's personal promise of presence and keeping to Jacob. On "I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest," the grammatical voices note the presence. Gill and Calvin note that "I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest" — the LORD gave Jacob the great personal promise of his presence ("I am with thee") and keeping (protection in all his journeys); the comfort to the lonely fugitive (vv.10-11); the covenant-presence (as to Isaac, 26:24); the assurance of God's protecting care wherever he went.

On "will bring thee again into this land," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the return. Wesley and Gill note the promise "will bring thee again into this land" — the assurance that Jacob, though now leaving (into exile), would be brought back to the land of promise; the comfort of a safe return (fulfilled, ch. 33; 35); the providence guaranteeing the homecoming.

On "I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faithfulness: "I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of" — God's faithful promise never to forsake Jacob until all was fulfilled; the perseverance of God's care; the assurance that God would perform all his word (the great comfort, Hebrews 13:5, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee); the covenant-faithfulness to the heir. The verse displays the LORD's promise of presence and keeping.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the presence — "I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest," the personal promise of presence and protection (26:24, Gill, Calvin); "will bring thee again into this land," the safe return (ch. 35, Wesley, Gill); "I will not leave thee, until I have done... I have spoken," the faithfulness (Hebrews 13:5, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promising LORD. The verse marks the presence: I am with thee. The verse displays the keeping: in all places. The verse displays the return: assured. The verse displays the faithfulness: I will not leave thee. The promise displays God's covenant-care for the fugitive. The verse displays the LORD: promising. The verse displays the Jacob: kept. The verse displays the faithfulness: pledged. The LORD's personal promise of presence and keeping to Jacob displays the covenant-comfort to the fugitive: I am with thee, will keep thee, bring thee again, and not leave thee till I have done all (26:24; Hebrews 13:5) — the great promise of God's presence, protection, return, and faithfulness to the lonely heir. The verse displays the comforted Jacob, who awakes in awe (vv.16-17).
16:
"And Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not." The witnesses note Jacob's awakening to the sense of God's presence. On "Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place," the grammatical voices note the realization. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place" — Jacob, waking from the dream (vv.12-15), realized that the LORD was present in that place (where he had met him); the sense of God's special presence; the awe of the divine manifestation; the recognition that God was there.

On "and I knew it not," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the ignorance. Henry and Calvin note Jacob's "and I knew it not" — he had not known, when he lay down (a common place, a stone for a pillow, v.11), that God would meet him there; the unexpected manifestation; the lesson that God is present where we know it not; the wonder at the unlooked-for visitation.

On the realization, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the presence: Jacob's "Surely the LORD is in this place" expresses the sense of God's special, manifest presence (not that God is absent elsewhere, but that he had here revealed himself); the awe; the recognition that turned the common place into the house of God (v.17); the comfort and wonder of the visited fugitive. The verse displays Jacob's awakening to the sense of God's presence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the realization — "Surely the LORD is in this place," Jacob sensing God's presence (Ellicott, Gill); "and I knew it not," the unexpected manifestation (Henry, Calvin); the awe at the visitation (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the awakened Jacob. The verse marks the awaking: from sleep. The verse displays the LORD: in this place. The verse displays the ignorance: I knew it not. The verse displays the awe: at the presence. The awakening displays Jacob sensing God's manifest presence. The verse displays the Jacob: awaking. The verse displays the LORD: present. The verse displays the wonder: felt. Jacob's awakening to the sense of God's presence displays the wonder of the visitation: surely the LORD is in this place, and I knew it not — Jacob realizing, on waking from the dream, that God had met him in the common place where he lay (v.11), the unlooked-for manifestation. The verse displays the awed Jacob, who calls it the house of God (v.17).
17:
"And he was afraid, and said, How dreadful is this place! this is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven." The witnesses note Jacob's awe — the house of God and the gate of heaven. On "he was afraid, and said, How dreadful is this place!" interpreters note the holy fear. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading note that Jacob "was afraid" — filled with an awe of the greatness and glory of God (the reverent fear that the manifestation of God's presence produces, Exodus 3:5-6; the holy dread, not slavish terror); "How dreadful (awe-inspiring, to be reverenced) is this place!" — the place made awful by God's presence; the reverent fear of the visited fugitive.

On "this is none other but the house of God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the house of God. Calvin and Gill note that Jacob called the place "the house of God (Bethel, v.19)" — the place where God had manifested himself, a house/dwelling of God (not that God dwells in a place, but that here he had revealed his presence); the place of God's special manifestation; the sanctuary-sense (the place hallowed by God's presence).

On "this is the gate of heaven," the grammatical-historical note the figure: "this is the gate of heaven" — the place a gate/door of heaven (where heaven seemed opened, the ladder reaching up, vv.12; the angels passing; the communion of heaven and earth opened); the figure of the place of God's manifestation and of access to heaven (and, by some, pointing to Christ, the true gate, John 1:51; 10:9); the awe at the opened heaven. The verse displays Jacob's awe — the house of God and the gate of heaven.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob, afraid, called the place the house of God and the gate of heaven (Clarke, Calvin, Gill). His awe bears its weight as a crux: the holy fear at the manifestation of God's presence (Exodus 3:5-6); "the house of God" (Bethel, v.19), the place hallowed by God's special manifestation; "the gate of heaven," the place where heaven was opened (the ladder, the angels, the communion of heaven and earth) — and, by some, a figure of Christ, the true gate (John 1:51). The verse displays the house-of-God crux. The verse marks the fear: dreadful place. The verse displays the house: of God. The verse displays the gate: of heaven. The verse displays the presence: manifest. The awe displays Jacob's holy fear at God's presence. The verse displays the Jacob: afraid. The verse displays the place: hallowed. The verse displays the heaven: opened. Jacob's awe — the house of God and the gate of heaven — displays the holy fear and a crux: how dreadful is this place! this is the house of God and the gate of heaven — the reverent fear at God's manifest presence (Exodus 3:5-6), the place hallowed (Bethel, v.19), the gate where heaven was opened (the ladder, the angels), pointing, by some, to Christ the true gate (John 1:51). The verse displays the awed Jacob, who sets up the pillar (v.18).
18:
"And Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone that he had put for his pillows, and set it up for a pillar, and poured oil upon the top of it." The witnesses note Jacob setting up and anointing the pillar. On "Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone... and set it up for a pillar," the grammatical voices note the memorial. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jacob rose up early... and took the stone that he had put for his pillows, and set it up for a pillar" — Jacob, early, took the stone of his pillow (v.11) and set it up as a pillar (a memorial, a standing monument of the vision and the place); the marking of the sacred spot; the commemoration of God's appearing.

On "poured oil upon the top of it," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the anointing. JFB and Gill note that Jacob "poured oil upon the top of it" — anointing/consecrating the pillar (a token of dedication to God, setting it apart as sacred); the consecration of the memorial; the act of worship and dedication (the oil a sign of consecration, as later the anointing of holy things); the hallowing of the spot.

On the pillar, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the worship: the pillar and the oil are an act of grateful worship and dedication — Jacob consecrating the place of God's appearing; the memorial of the covenant-vision; the spot marked and hallowed (Bethel, v.19); the patriarch's response of worship to the manifestation. The verse displays Jacob setting up and anointing the pillar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the memorial — "took the stone... and set it up for a pillar," Jacob's memorial of the vision (v.11, Ellicott, Gill); "poured oil upon the top of it," the anointing/consecration (JFB, Gill); the act of grateful worship (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the consecrating Jacob. The verse marks the stone: a pillar. The verse displays the oil: poured. The verse displays the consecration: of the spot. The verse displays the worship: rendered. The act displays Jacob commemorating and consecrating the place. The verse displays the Jacob: setting up. The verse displays the pillar: anointed. The verse displays the place: hallowed. Jacob setting up and anointing the pillar displays the memorial and consecration of the place: Jacob set up the stone of his pillow for a pillar and poured oil on it (v.11) — the grateful commemoration and consecration of the spot of God's appearing, the response of worship (Bethel, v.19). The verse displays the worshiping Jacob, who names the place (v.19).
19:
"And he called the name of that place Bethel: but the name of that city was called Luz at the first." The witnesses note the naming of Bethel. On "he called the name of that place Bethel," the grammatical voices note the name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he called the name of that place Bethel" — Jacob named the place Bethel ("house of God," v.17); the name commemorating God's appearing and the sacredness of the spot; the memorial of the vision; the place that would be notable in Israel's history (35:1-15; later a sanctuary, and sadly a place of idolatry, 1 Kings 12:29).

On "Bethel," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the meaning. Clarke and Ellicott note that "Bethel" means "house of God" (v.17) — the name fitting the place of God's manifestation; the memorial of the visitation; the hallowed spot named for God's presence.

On "the name of that city was called Luz at the first," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the old name: "the name of that city was called Luz at the first" — the place (or the nearby city) was formerly called Luz (almond, or hazel); Jacob renamed it Bethel; the change of name marking the new significance (the house of God); the providence hallowing the once-common place. The verse displays the naming of Bethel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "he called the name of that place Bethel," the house of God named (v.17, Keil, Gill); "Bethel," the house of God (Clarke, Ellicott); "the name of that city was called Luz at the first," the old name (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Jacob. The verse marks the name: Bethel. The verse displays the meaning: house of God. The verse displays the Luz: the old name. The verse displays the memorial: of the vision. The naming displays Jacob commemorating God's appearing. The verse displays the Jacob: naming. The verse displays the Bethel: hallowed. The verse displays the place: renamed. The naming of Bethel displays the memorial of God's appearing: Jacob called the place Bethel (house of God, v.17), formerly Luz — the once-common place hallowed and renamed for God's manifestation, notable in Israel's history (35:1-15). The verse displays the naming Jacob, who vows a vow (vv.20-22).
20:
"And Jacob vowed a vow, saying, If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on," The witnesses note Jacob's vow — the crux of its character. On "Jacob vowed a vow," the grammatical voices note the first vow. Gill and Clarke note that "Jacob vowed a vow" — the first vow recorded in Scripture; a vow being a solemn, voluntary promise to God, made (Clarke notes) with prayer; the patriarch binding himself to God; the response of faith and devotion to the vision (vv.12-15).

On "If God will be with me, and will keep me... and will give me bread... and raiment," interpreters weigh the conditional form. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that Jacob's vow is framed conditionally — "If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way... and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on" (and bring me home, v.21); the crux: is this a low, mercenary bargain (Jacob haggling with God, making his service conditional on benefits), or the language of faith? The dominant reading holds it is not a bargaining condition but the laying hold of God's own promise: the "if" echoes the very things God had just promised (I am with thee, will keep thee, will bring thee again, v.15) — Jacob takes God at his word, vowing to respond if (as he believes) God does what he has said; the vow of faith, asking only necessities (bread and raiment, cf. 1 Timothy 6:8) and pledging devotion; not a doubting or grasping condition, but the modest, believing response of the pilgrim to the promise.

On "bread to eat, and raiment to put on," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the modesty: Jacob asked only the necessities — bread and raiment (the content of godly contentment, 1 Timothy 6:8; Matthew 6:25-33); the moderation of his desire; the vow of one content with God's provision; the believing, humble petition. The verse displays Jacob's vow.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob vowed, conditioning his pledge on God's being with him, keeping him, providing, and bringing him home (Gill, Calvin, Clarke). The vow bears its weight as a crux of its character: the conditional "if" read not as a mercenary bargain but as the language of faith — Jacob laying hold of God's own just-given promise (v.15, I am with thee, will keep thee, will bring thee again), asking only necessities (bread and raiment, 1 Timothy 6:8), and pledging devotion; the modest, believing response of the pilgrim, not a haggling with God. The verse displays the vow crux. The verse marks the vow: the first. The verse displays the debate: a bargain, or the vow of faith. The verse displays the conditions: God's own promises (v.15). The verse displays the modesty: bread and raiment. The vow displays Jacob's believing response to the promise. The verse displays the Jacob: vowing. The verse displays the faith: laying hold. The verse displays the necessities: only asked. Jacob's vow displays a crux: if God will be with me, keep me, and give me bread and raiment — read not as a mercenary bargain but as the language of faith, laying hold of God's own promise (v.15), asking only necessities (1 Timothy 6:8), and pledging devotion. The verse displays the vowing Jacob, who pledges his God and his tithe (vv.21-22).
21:
"So that I come again to my father's house in peace; then shall the LORD be my God:" The witnesses note Jacob's vow that the LORD shall be his God. On "So that I come again to my father's house in peace," the grammatical voices note the return. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "so that I come again to my father's house in peace" — the last of the conditions Jacob named: a safe return to his father's house (echoing God's promise, v.15, I will bring thee again into this land); the desire for a peaceful homecoming; the laying hold of God's word; the pilgrim's hope of return.

On "then shall the LORD be my God," interpreters expound the pledge. The reformed Calvin and witnesses note that "then shall the LORD be my God" — Jacob's vow to take the LORD (Jehovah, the covenant-God who had appeared, v.13) as his God; not that he would only then begin to worship God (he already feared God), but a solemn, personal, covenantal taking of the LORD as his own God; the appropriating of the covenant-God by faith; the personal commitment of the pilgrim; the response of the heart to God's promise (the LORD his God, in covenant, Exodus 6:7).

On the vow, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the faith: Jacob's "then shall the LORD be my God" is the language of personal, covenantal faith — taking God at his word (v.15) and binding himself to him; not a mercenary condition but the believing appropriation of the covenant-God; the personal devotion of the heir. The verse displays Jacob's vow that the LORD shall be his God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "so that I come again to my father's house in peace," the safe homecoming (v.15, Keil, Gill); "then shall the LORD be my God," the personal taking of the covenant-God (Calvin, Gill); the believing appropriation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vowing Jacob. The verse marks the return: in peace. The verse displays the LORD: his God. The verse displays the covenant: appropriated. The verse displays the faith: personal. The vow displays Jacob taking the LORD as his own God. The verse displays the Jacob: vowing. The verse displays the LORD: his God. The verse displays the commitment: personal. Jacob's vow that the LORD shall be his God displays the personal, covenantal faith: so I come home in peace, then shall the LORD be my God (v.15) — the believing appropriation of the covenant-God (Exodus 6:7), the personal commitment of the pilgrim to the God who had promised. The verse displays the committed Jacob, who pledges the pillar and the tithe (v.22).
22:
"And this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God's house: and of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee." The witnesses note Jacob's vow of the pillar and the tithe. On "this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God's house," the grammatical voices note the dedication. Gill and Clarke note that "this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God's house" — Jacob vowed that the pillar (and the place, Bethel) should be a house of God (a place of worship); the dedication of the spot to God's service (fulfilled when Jacob returned and built an altar there, 35:7); the consecration of the place; the memorial to become a sanctuary.

On "of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee," interpreters note the tithe. The reformed Calvin and witnesses note that Jacob vowed "I will surely give the tenth unto thee" — to give God a tenth (tithe) of all that God gave him; the dedication of a portion to God (as Abraham gave tithes to Melchizedek, 14:20); the acknowledgment that all is of God, and the returning of a portion in worship and gratitude; the pledge of devotion and thankful giving.

On the vow, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the devotion: Jacob's vow (the pillar a house of God, and the tithe) is the response of grateful faith — the dedication of the place and a portion of his substance to God; the worship and thankfulness of the pilgrim; the binding of himself to God's service; the proper, devout vow (not a bargain, but a grateful pledge). The verse displays Jacob's vow of the pillar and the tithe.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dedication — "this stone... shall be God's house," the place dedicated (35:7, Gill, Clarke); "I will surely give the tenth unto thee," the tithe vowed (14:20, Calvin, Gill); the grateful response of devotion (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vowing Jacob. The verse marks the pillar: God's house. The verse displays the tithe: a tenth. The verse displays the dedication: of place and substance. The verse displays the devotion: grateful. The vow displays Jacob dedicating the place and a portion to God. The verse displays the Jacob: vowing. The verse displays the pillar: a house of God. The verse displays the tithe: pledged. Jacob's vow of the pillar and the tithe displays the grateful devotion of faith: this pillar shall be God's house, and I will give the tenth of all (14:20; 35:7) — the dedication of the place and a portion of his substance to God, the worship and thankful giving of the pilgrim, the binding of himself to God's service. The verse closes the chapter, before Jacob's arrival at Padan-aram (ch.29).
